[0:04] What's up geeks and welcome to the channel! In the first video of this series we are going
[0:10] to start with one of the simplest yet one of the most used design
[0:14] patterns out there: The Singleton Pattern. The singleton is a creational design pattern
[0:20] this pattern ensures that only one instance of its kind exists
[0:24] and provides a single point of access to it from any other part of our application
[0:30] As a real-world analogy take the government of a country or its president,
[0:34] you see a given country can have only one official government or one official president
[0:39] regardless of the individuals who form this government or the person that
[0:43] is currently the president of this country, so the title "the government or the president
[0:48] of X" is a global identifier it is a global point of access
[0:53] used to represent or reference the president or the individuals in charge of this country.
[0:58] Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
[1:04] your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the

chunks.text:
[0:58] Singletons represent exactly the same idea, just like global variables they let you access
[1:04] your object from anywhere in your application but on top of that, they encapsulate the
[1:09] attributes of this object in one single class and guarantee that only one instance of this class
[1:16] will be available at any point in time but why would anyone want to control
[1:21] how many instances a class has? Well think of a database instance,
[1:26] only one instance of a database is required and should be used by all the parts of our application
[1:31] So instead of creating a new instance every time we need to query our database
[1:36] we create a singleton database instance and reuse this same instance every time we access our tables
[1:42] by doing this we didn't only guarantee a faster access and retrieval of the instance
[1:47] but we also reduced the possibility of encountering an error or exception
[1:52] while creating these several instances.
[1:54] Now let's go ahead and try to implement the Singleton class together.
[1:58] The first thing we need to do is create a private static field inside our singleton,

[2:12] can be the username and password needed to connect for example or anything else

[2:22] make sure that the access level of this constructor is set to private
[2:26] inside this constructor we can go ahead and initialize all the attributes of our object
[2:30] in our case we only have one the "data" field. This constructor was made private because we
[2:35] only need access to it using a public static method created inside the singleton class
[2:42] usually this method is named getInstance() to refer to the same single instance it returns
[2:48] so this method should return an instance of the Singleton class
[2:51] the instance we declared at the beginning of our implementation

[2:42] usually this method is named getInstance() to refer to the same single instance it returns
[2:48] so this method should return an instance of the Singleton class
[2:51] the instance we declared at the beginning of our implementation
[2:54] but how do we guarantee that the same instance is returned every single time?
[2:59] Well the created instance is wrapped inside an
[3:03] if-statement that checks if the static instance of our singleton is null or not
[3:08] and only if it is we proceed by creating a new instance of it using our private constructor
[3:14] however if it isn't we return the same instance already stored inside our Singleton class.
[3:20] Now some of you may say that the code we wrote isn't robust enough
[3:23] because if we were dealing with multi-threaded environments
[3:26] nothing in this code prevents two threads from accessing this piece of code at the same time
[3:31] and return with two different instances of this same Singleton class
[3:35] and to these people I say you're right! To solve this issue and as discussed
[3:40] in our previous videos, all we have to do is wrap

[4:13] and only when it is their time to go they will check if the instance
[4:16] is available and then return with it This problem is actually not specific
[4:21] to the Singleton pattern and can be solved by applying
[4:24] the double-checked locking idiom on our method How do we do that? Well, we simply wrap

[5:10] Okay intuitively this piece of code may seem like a complete and efficient solution to the
[5:15] multi-threading problem we were mentioning However it is still missing something.
[5:20] Consider we have two threads in our application trying to access this method
[5:24] threads A and B. Now thread A is the first to access the method
[5:28] and it notices that the instance of our singleton is not initialized
[5:33] therefore it obtains the lock and begins to initialize the value
[5:36] Now due to the semantics of some programming languages
[5:39] the code generated by the compiler is allowed to update the shared variable
[5:44] stored in the memory to reference a partially constructed object
[5:48] So before thread a finishes the whole initialization process
[5:52] the shared variable or the singleton in our case can be immediately updated in the memory
[5:57] and other threads of our application will consider it as initialized and use it.
[6:02] In our example while this object is being constructed by thread A,
[6:06] thread B may try to retrieve it and because of the first if-statement we just added
chunks.textPreview: [5:10] Okay intuitively this piece of code may seem like a complete and efficient solution to the [5:15] multi-threading problem we were mentioning However it is still missing something. [5:20] Consider we have two threads in our application trying to access this method [5:24] th...

chunks.title: Saved YouTube Video
chunks.text:
[6:02] In our example while this object is being constructed by thread A,
[6:06] thread B may try to retrieve it and because of the first if-statement we just added
[6:11] thread B will not wait for thread A to finish the initialization
[6:14] and will use the instance referenced even if it is a partially constructed object
[6:19] causing our application to crash. To avoid such scenarios all we
[6:24] have to do is set the singleton instance we have to be volatile.
[6:29] This keyword ensures that multiple threads will be able to handle the singleton instance correctly.
[6:34] If you have no idea what volatile fields are or if you haven't made use of this keyword before
[6:39] feel free to check the video linked down below in the description.
[6:42] Now this, is a complete and working solution for the singleton pattern
[6:47] Additionally there is still kind of a micro-optimization we can do to enhance
[6:52] the performance of this implementation. You see the variable we have is
[6:56] now marked as volatile so every time we access
[6:59] this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached

[6:52] the performance of this implementation. You see the variable we have is
[6:56] now marked as volatile so every time we access
[6:59] this variable we need to read it directly from the main memory as it cannot be cached
[7:03] So in this single code snippet even if the variable was already initialized
[7:09] we have to fetch it twice the first time in the
[7:12] if-statement to check if it is null and the second time in the return
[7:15] statement to return it to the user. To avoid doing this and access the
[7:19] memory only once what we can do is
[7:21] store this instance in a local variable the first time we retrieve it from the memory
[7:26] then use this same local variable in both the if-statement and the return statement.
[7:31] The same logic and way of thinking can be expanded and used inside the synchronized block
[7:36] to avoid multiple direct reads of the memory. This simple usage of such a local variable
[7:42] can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
[7:48] So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single

[7:42] can and will improve the methods overall performance by as much as 40%
[7:48] So to recap we can say that the Singleton Pattern should be used when a class must have a single
[7:54] instance available to all its clients as this pattern disables all other means
[7:59] of creating objects of a class except for the special static creation method
[8:04] This method either creates a new object or returns the existing one if it has already been created
[8:10] However this pattern requires special treatment in a multi-threaded environment as its code needs
[8:15] to be adapted to handle multiple threads. So that's it for this video, I hope it was
[8:21] helpful, thank you guys for watching, take care, and I will see you in the next one!
